---
title: "3.5 Kalman Filter as Market-Making Model"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
notes: |
  Once the Kalman filter is understood as a recursive fair-value estimator, it
  no longer belongs only to pair trading. The same machinery can update the
  hidden mean of one asset from recent trades, which is exactly the kind of
  problem a market maker faces.

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    menu: false
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
filters:
  - ../../../../scripts/lua/remove-reveal-notes.lua
include-before-body:
  - ../../../../styles/reveal-audience-only.html
execute:
  echo: true
---

## The same Kalman logic can estimate one asset's fair value, not just a pair hedge ratio.

| Pair version            | Market-making version               |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| hidden state = slope/intercept | hidden state = fair value mean |
| observable = paired price | observable = transaction price     |

::: {.notes}
Start by showing continuity rather than novelty. We are not learning a new
algorithm; we are specializing the same algorithm to a simpler hidden state.
:::

## Trade size changes how much we should trust the latest observed price.

| Small trade             | Large trade                          |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| more measurement noise  | less measurement noise               |
| update mean cautiously  | update mean aggressively             |

::: {.notes}
This is the practical twist that makes the model market-making relevant. The
latest print is not equally informative at all sizes.
:::

## Kalman fair value behaves like a volume-and-time-weighted moving mean.

::: {.notes}
Close the section by linking it to a familiar intuition: VWAP-like averaging,
but with explicit recursion and a dynamic uncertainty update.
:::
